excerpt: The Fatimid tradition reads the saying 'I was a prophet while Ādam was still between water and clay' as a charter for the priority of the Muḥammadan light over the formal creation of the first man, by which the prophetic substance is shown to be the inward principle of which Ādam is the outward vessel.
source_location_hint: discussions of the priority of the prophetic light and the cycles of nuṭaqāʾ

excerpt: In Ismaili taʾwīl, Ādam's recovery through invocation of the shahāda inscribed upon the Throne signifies the dependence of the first nāṭiq's repentance and reinstatement upon the inner reality of the seal of the prophets, so that the human soul's return is mediated by recognition of the Muḥammadan witness.
source_location_hint: taʾwīl of the story of Ādam and the angels' prostration

excerpt: The Nāṭiq of each cycle, called Ādam, is the appointed inaugurator who opens a new dawr under the Imam of his time, while the Qāʾim seals the cycle at its end.
source_location_hint: section on the cycle of Ādam as inaugurating nāṭiq of the dawr

excerpt: The earthly daʿwa is sustained by a hierarchy of twelve ḥudūd, ranged beneath the Imam as nuqabāʾ and ḥujjas, mirroring in the world below the structure of the celestial intellects above.
source_location_hint: on the correspondence of the cosmic ḥudūd and the earthly ranks of the daʿwa

excerpt: The prophet receives taʾyīd (divine support and inspiration) from the higher ḥudūd, so that what he lacks in acquired learning is supplied by the inward illumination conferred upon him from above.
source_location_hint: section on the nature of prophetic support (taʾyīd) and its role in completing the Nāṭiq's formation

excerpt: What the angels manifested in their question 'Will You place therein one who works corruption?' was an outward objection; what they concealed in their hearts was their own sense of superior fitness for the office.
source_location_hint: chapter on the taʾwīl of the story of Ādam and the questioning of the angels

excerpt: The angels, after their repentance, were commanded to descend and build a House on earth as a place of forgiveness for the erring children of Ādam, corresponding to their own ṭawāf of the Throne.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Ḥajj; taʾwīl of the Kaʿba and its origins

excerpt: Each ḥadd in the hierarchy of the daʿwa stands in a graded relation of nearness to and dependence upon the rank above it, such that the lowest of a higher circle remains superior to the highest of the circle beneath it, and the possibility of lapse pertains only to the lower ranks under the law of the Nāṭiq.
source_authority: Hujjat of Iraq, leading Fatimid theologian (d. after 411/1020)
source_location_hint: Sections on the structure of ḥudūd and the gradation of the spiritual hierarchy

excerpt: Iblīs's claim of superiority — fire over clay — is glossed as the boast of one schooled in the abstract sciences refusing submission to the Nāṭiq, in whom the abstract truths are joined to the composite sciences of sharīʿa and their taʾwīl.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the angelic prostration and the rank of the Nāṭiq

excerpt: The twelve ribs from which the wife of Ādam was fashioned correspond to the twelve ḥujjas or nuqabāʾ of the Nāṭiq, upon whom the stability of the daʿwa rests as the body's stability rests upon its ribs.
source_location_hint: Chapter on the cycle of Ādam and the creation of Ḥawwāʾ

excerpt: The 'day of the appointed time' (yawm al-waqt al-maʿlūm) signifies the manifestation of the Qāʾim of the Resurrection, in whose cycle the inward truths of religion are disclosed.
source_authority: Nasir-i Khusraw (Fatimid-era Ismaili philosopher and dāʿī)
source_location_hint: Discourses on the eschatological cycle of the Qāʾim

excerpt: The four sides from which Iblīs approaches (before, behind, right, left) correspond to the avenues of doubt by which he assails the seeker on the straight path; he is barred only from above and below, which represent the descent of divine support and the foundation of the imāmate.
source_location_hint: Discussion of the satanic obstruction of the seeker upon ṣirāṭ mustaqīm

excerpt: Iblīs's counter-summons operates by mimicry of the daʿwa: he establishes ranks, deploys his own duʿāt, and counterfeits the language of guidance to draw souls away from the Imām of the time.
source_location_hint: Majālis on the parallel structure of the satanic daʿwa

excerpt: In the taʾwīl of Q 17:64, 'wealth' (amwāl) signifies the inner knowledge whose purity Iblīs seeks to corrupt through doubt, and 'children' (awlād) signifies the spiritual offspring of the daʿwa whom he labours to estrange from love of the Imāms.
source_authority: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān (chief jurist of the Fatimid daʿwa, d. 363/974)
source_location_hint: Taʾwīl of the satanic share in amwāl and awlād

excerpt: For every nāṭiq, every waṣī, and every Imām, God has appointed an adversary from among the satans of mankind and jinn, so that the contest between the two summonses may be made manifest in each cycle.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the pairing of prophet and adversary in every era

excerpt: In Fatimid taʾwīl, the serpent's whisper is read as the suggestion of a counterfeit summons (daʿwa muḍilla) addressed to the lower receptive faculty; the false oath is the deceiver's signature, since in the precinct of true gnosis falsehood under oath is structurally impossible and therefore disarming when encountered.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qadi (qadi al-quḍāt) under al-Muʿizz li-Din Allah
source_location_hint: taʾwīl on the story of Ādam (Q7:20–22)

excerpt: The luminous garment that clothes the prophet in his upper station is the radiance of taʾyīd; its withdrawal does not annul the prophetic office but unveils the prophet's lower veil to the gaze of those beneath him, so that the imperfection visible there serves as the very occasion of instruction for the respondents.
source_authority: Fatimid dāʿī of Khurāsān, fourth/tenth century
source_location_hint: chapters on the conditions of prophetic transmission

excerpt: The believer's repentance is itself a station of nearness: the slip of one foot is the occasion for the other foot's firmness, so that the lapse becomes the door to a more rooted return to the Lord.
source_author: Imam ʿAli ibn al-Husayn Zayn al-ʿAbidin
source_authority: fourth Imam, recognised across Shiʿi traditions as a primary source for the etiquette of repentance
source_location_hint: the supplication on confession and seeking repentance

excerpt: The sciences of the Qāʾim are sealed within the cycle of concealment and become accessible only at the unveiling (kashf); to reach for them prematurely is to overstep the rank assigned to one's own cycle, and constitutes the very paradigm of the Ādamic lapse in its esoteric reading.
source_authority: foremost Fatimid philosopher-dāʿī, Hujjat al-ʿIraqayn under al-Hakim
source_location_hint: discussion of the cycles of concealment and unveiling

excerpt: Every higher rank within the daʿwa hierarchy stands as a Garden to the rank beneath it, and every lower rank stands as an earth to the rank above it; descent through these ranks is therefore intelligible as the same drama enacted at different scales, with the absolute Garden and the absolute earth as the limits of the series.
source_authority: foremost Fatimid philosopher-dāʿī, Hujjat al-ʿIraqayn under al-Hakim
source_location_hint: the seventh mashraʿ on the ranks of the daʿwa

excerpt: The respondent (mustajīb) is bound by the outward command of the sharīʿa and is not yet a partaker of the inner science; the duty of the duʿāt is to draw him upward by graduated instruction, so that the law itself becomes the staircase by which he ascends into the meaning of the law.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qadi under al-Muʿizz li-Din Allah
source_location_hint: the book of walāya, on the ranks of the believers

excerpt: The Second Emanator (al-Munbaʿith al-Thānī) holds his being from the First Emanator and is forbidden to claim equality with him; the conceit of rank-parity is the originating lapse in the world of intellects, mirrored downward as every Ādamic transgression in the lower worlds.
source_authority: foremost Fatimid philosopher-dāʿī, Hujjat al-ʿIraqayn under al-Hakim
source_location_hint: the chapters on al-Munbaʿithūn and the order of the spiritual ranks

excerpt: The seven words by which Ādam was returned to the Garden correspond to the seven intellects that had passed beyond him in his fall; his repentance is therefore a re-cognition of the hierarchy he had transgressed, and his restoration is the restoration of order in the spiritual world.
source_authority: foremost Fatimid philosopher-dāʿī, Hujjat al-ʿIraqayn under al-Hakim
source_location_hint: the seventh mashraʿ on the order of the intellects and the return

excerpt: The repentance that brings the servant back is the repentance unalloyed with persistence; for whoever persists is not as one who returns, and whoever returns is not as one who insists.
source_author: Imam ʿAli b. Abi Talib (compiled by al-Sharif al-Radi)
source_location_hint: Sermons and aphorisms on tawba

excerpt: The Imam in every age is the one through whose mediation God accepts the repentance of His servants, for he is the gate (bab) through which one approaches the Lord.
source_location_hint: On the function of the Imam as intermediary in tawba and istighfar

excerpt: The hudud of religion are arranged in a descending order, each receiving from the one above it and conveying to the one below it, so that the light of taʾyid reaches the believer only through the mediation of the limit nearest to him.
source_location_hint: On the correspondence of the upper (ʿalawi) and lower (sufli) hudud

excerpt: Envy (hasad) is the first sin by which a servant opposed his Lord — the sin of Iblis — and the first sin by which the earth was stained, when one of the sons of Adam slew his brother out of envy.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Iman, chapter on the major sins (al-kabaʾir)

excerpt: The bismillah is the key of every sura and the inauguration of every act; its mamthul in the order of the prophets is Adam, who opened the first cycle and in whom the letters of the divine name found their first human bearer.
source_location_hint: On the taʾwil of the bismillah and its correspondence with Adam

excerpt: Despair (qunut) of the mercy of God is itself among the gravest of sins, for it denies the very attribute by which He sustains His creation; the believer who has fallen must turn back at once, for the door of repentance is never closed so long as the breath remains.
source_location_hint: Closing exhortation on tawba and the awliyaʾ as mediators of forgiveness
